coredata.dc:title: Paper based analytical devices for ions determination in nasal secretions demonstrating association with olfactory function

Abdelazim, Mohamed H.

Abduljabbar, Maram H.
Almalki, Atiah H.
coredata.dc:description: Nasal ions environment plays a crucial role in maintaining nasal physiology and supports olfactory transmission. Addressing the limited research on nasal ion levels and their association with olfactory function, paper-based sensors were developed for determination of sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride in the nasal mucus of healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory dysfunction. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes and carbon quantum dots from beetroot were incorporated into paper substrate where sensors were designed with ion association complexes for sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride enhancing the recognition sensing capabilities. The sensors composition was optimized, including ion-exchange materials and plasticizers, to enhance sensitivity and selectivity. The performance of the sensors is evaluated based on Nernstian slope, dynamic range, detection limit and response time. Selectivity of the sensors was tested and the results demonstrated high selectivity for the target ions. The sensors were successfully determined sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride levels in nasal mucus of healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory dysfunction. The results revealed elevated calcium levels in patients with olfactory dysfunction, highlighting associated diagnostic implications. This suggests that the proposed sensors could serve as a diagnostic tool for olfactory evaluation, particularly in resource-constrained settings where access to advanced diagnostic tools is limited.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Paper-based sensors
Olfactory dysfunction
Ion composition
Carbon nanotubes
Carbon quantum dots

Synthesis of CQDs from beetroot slices
Preparation of ink solution containing MWCNT and CQDs
Design of the modified paper based sensors for the investigated ions
Design of filter paper strips modified with MWCNT and CQDs

Determination of sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride in the human nasal secretions of healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory dysfunction
Investigation of the modified paper substrate coated with ink consisted of MWCNT and CQDs
Rational for choosing paper as a platform for the proposed analysis
Transformation of filter paper into conductive platform: enhancing electrical conductivity and performance with nanoparticles inking process
Assessment of the coated paper surface characters and the conductivity features
Optimization of the components of the proposed sensors
Performance evaluation of the described sensors
Application of the proposed method for determination of sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride in the nasal secretions of healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory dysfunction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic description for the steps of the paper-based sensor design.
Characterization of the carbon quantum dots using XRD pattern (a), TEM image (b), FTIR spectrum (c).

FESEM image of filter paper coated with graphite ink(a), filter paper coated with MWCNT and carbon quantum dots (b), and electrochemical impedance spectra of the graphite coated paper and paper after modification with MWCNT and carbon quantum dots (c).
Optimization of the composition of the sensing ingredients.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Bold values represent the optimum compositions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Method performance and validation data obtained using the proposed sensors.
Assessment of sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride in the nasal secretions of healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory dysfunction.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Bold values indicated significant higher levels of calcium in patients with olfactory dysfunction.

head.ce:title: Paper based analytical devices for ions determination in nasal secretions demonstrating association with olfactory function

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nasal ions environment plays a crucial role in maintaining nasal physiology and supports olfactory transmission. Addressing the limited research on nasal ion levels and their association with olfactory function, paper-based sensors were developed for determination of sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride in the nasal mucus of healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory dysfunction. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes and carbon quantum dots from beetroot were incorporated into paper substrate where sensors were designed with ion association complexes for sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride enhancing the recognition sensing capabilities. The sensors composition was optimized, including ion-exchange materials and plasticizers, to enhance sensitivity and selectivity. The performance of the sensors is evaluated based on Nernstian slope, dynamic range, detection limit and response time. Selectivity of the sensors was tested and the results demonstrated high selectivity for the target ions. The sensors were successfully determined sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride levels in nasal mucus of healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory dysfunction. The results revealed elevated calcium levels in patients with olfactory dysfunction, highlighting associated diagnostic implications. This suggests that the proposed sensors could serve as a diagnostic tool for olfactory evaluation, particularly in resource-constrained settings where access to advanced diagnostic tools is limited.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nasal secretions, commonly known as nasal mucus, are crucial for the proper functioning of the nasal cavity and respiratory system [1,2]. These secretions comprise a complex mixture of water, proteins, mucins, lipids, and electrolytes. Although ions make up only 1 % of nasal secretions, they play a significant role in maintaining osmotic balance, regulating pH, and ensuring hydration levels within the nasal cavity [3,4]. Analyzing the ionic composition of biological fluids has become vital in biomedical research and clinical diagnostics. The ionic makeup of the sensory cleft is particularly important in transmitting olfactory information from the nasal cavity to the central processing system [5]. Various studies have suggested that elevated calcium levels in the nasal secretions of patients with olfactory dysfunction may desensitize olfactory receptor neurons and negatively impact odor transmission [6–8]. Additionally, Selvaraj et al. found that chronic inflammation of the sinonasal mucosa in murine models alters sodium and potassium levels in olfactory secretions compared to those without chronic inflammation [9].

Particularly, investigating the levels of essential ions, such as sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride, has garnered significant attention, as they represent the main ions in nasal mucus. Reviewing the literature reveals a lack of complete investigation into ion levels in the nasal cavity and their association with olfactory dysfunction. Accurate determination of ion levels in medical settings provides an attractive approach for diagnosis and routine examination. The development of efficient point-of-care diagnostic devices that enable easy detection of biological markers for diseases is essential. Portable paper-based analytical sensing plays a vital role in point-of-care testing, offering high sensitivity and rapid response while achieving advances in cost-reduction and automation, all while maintaining high standards of sensitivity and selectivity [10–13].

To date, no validated analytical study has accurately determined essential ions, particularly sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride in the nasal mucus of healthy individuals in comparison to patients with chronic olfactory dysfunctions. The objective of this research is to develop and evaluate cost-effective paper-based sensors modified with multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), synthesized from beetroot slices, for the determination of sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride ions in human nasal secretions. The sensors design is carefully developed, involving the preparation of ion association complexes for sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride to enhance the recognition properties and sensing capabilities of the investigated ions. The sensors are optimized and assessed in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, dynamic range, and response time. Furthermore, this research aims to investigate the ionic composition differences between healthy individuals and those with chronic olfactory dysfunction, offering a potential diagnostic tool for olfactory evaluation in resource-constrained settings. The paper-based analytical have several distinct advantages over traditional methods, including portability, affordability, and compatibility with low volume samples. By meeting the criteria outlined by WHO for analytical and diagnostic devices in resource-constrained settings, these devices hold promise in extending their benefits to regions where sophisticated and expensive diagnostic tools are not readily available.

The analytical-grade reagents were utilized along with freshly distilled water in the procedure. High molecular weight poly (vinyl chloride) (PVC), dioctyl phthalate (DOP), tri butyl phosphate (TBP), tetrahydrofuran, and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany). Sodium chloride, potassium chloride, calcium chloride, magnesium chloride, zinc chloride and sodium phosphate (El-Nasr Company, Egypt). Sodium hydroxide (El-Nasr Company, Egypt) was made into a 0.1 N aqueous solution, and hydrochloric acid (El-Nasr Company, Egypt) was made into a 0.1 N aqueous solution. Phosphotungstic acid (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), prepared as 10⁻2 M aqueous solution. 1,10-Phenanthroline (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), was employed to prepare iron(II)– Phenanthroline solution by dissolving 100 mg in 20 mL of 10−2 M iron(II) sulfate, followed by drops of ethanol and water to keep a clear solution. Carbon conductive paint (Garfield Ave, West Chester, PA, USA) was used. Dry fresh slices of beetroots were supplied by Abelazim Khamys private agricultural land, Kafrelshikh, Egypt.

The Jenway pH meter model 3510 (USA) was used. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired using a PerkinElmer FTIR spectrophotometer (USA). The surface of the paper substrate was examined using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM; QUANTA FEG 250, Netherlands). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) measurements of the sensor were performed using a Metrohom potentiostat/galvanostat. X-ray diffractometry (XRD) was carried out using a Bruker AXS-D8 instrument (Germany) with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 °A) in the 2θ range from 5° to 85° to analyze nanoparticles.

Standard stock solutions of sodium chloride, potassium chloride, and calcium chloride (10−1 M) were prepared by dissolving the weighted reference powder in 50 mL of distilled water and adjusting the volume to 100 mL with the solvent. Quality control samples of aqueous solutions for each ion were then prepared at various concentrations: the lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) for sodium was set at 5.5 × 10−7 M, for potassium at 8.0 × 10−7 M, for calcium at 8.5 × 10−7 M, and for chloride at 3.5 × 10−7 M. Additionally, lower quantifiable concentrations (LQC) of 10−6 M, middle quantifiable concentrations (MQC) of 10−3 M, and high quantifiable concentrations (HQC) of 10−1 M were prepared for all ions. These concentrations allowed for accurate and precise measurements during the analytical process for each specific ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Precipitation-based technique for the preparation of ion association complex of sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride
ce:section.ce:para: Ion association complexes, namely sodium phosphotungstate (Na-PT), potassium phosphotungstate (K-PT), and calcium phosphotungstate (Ca-PT), were created. 50 mL of 10−2 M phosphotungstic acid was combined with 150 ml of 10−2 M aqueous sodium, potassium, and calcium solutions. The formed precipitates were filtered, washed, dried, and ground to fine powder. The ion association complex of chloride with iron(II)-phenanthroline (Cl-IP) was formed by treating 50 mL of 10−2 M aqueous chloride solution with two drops of 10 % ammonia solution, then mixing it with 50 mL of 10−2 M iron(II)-phenanthroline solution. The resulting precipitate was filtered, washed, dried, and ground.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from beetroot slices
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh slices of beetroots were carbonized in an electric oven at 250 °C for 50 min. The obtained product was allowed to cool to room temperature before being ground into a fine powder. A weight equivalent to 400 mg of the powdered product was dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water. The solution was then subjected to a washing step using 20 mL of dichloromethane to eliminate any remaining organic components. The mixture was thoroughly mixed and then allowed to separate into two phases. The aqueous phase was carefully collected, and the organic phase was discarded. The resulting aqueous solution was subjected to centrifugation (g) at 3000 rpm for 15 min to remove any insoluble particles. The supernatant was collected and filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane filter to ensure the removal of fine particulates. To further refine the product, excess acetone (approximately 120 mL) was added to the upper brown solution to precipitate the carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The mixture was then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was discarded, and the precipitate was washed with 30 mL of acetone three times to remove any impurities. The washed precipitate was then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain the final product. This procedure yielded approximately 200 mg of beetroot-derived carbon quantum dots (beetroot-CQDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ink solution containing MWCNT and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare an ink solution with desirable electrochemical properties, 500 mg of MWCNT and 500 mg of gum Arabic were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water in a 100 mL volumetric flask. The solution was stirred vigorously using a magnetic stirrer for 30 min to ensure complete dissolution and dispersion of the MWCNT and gum Arabic. After this initial stirring, 5 mL of propylene glycol was added to the mixture. The solution was then stirred continuously for an additional 30 min to achieve a homogeneous mixture. Subsequently, 100 mg of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was added to the prepared solution. The resulting mixture was stirred for another 30 min to ensure thorough incorporation of the CQDs into the ink solution. The final ink solution was then filtered using a 0.45 μm membrane filter to remove any undissolved particles and ensure uniformity. The prepared ink solution was stored in a sealed container at room temperature until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the modified paper based sensors for the investigated ions
Design of filter paper strips modified with MWCNT and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To develop four conductive sensors, we used two rectangular filter paper strips, each measuring 20 × 5 mm, as a platform for each sensor. The ink solution containing MWCNT and CQDs was carefully applied to both sides of the paper strips using a soft brush. We then used two plastic insulator sheets, each with two 4 mm orifices on the top, to sandwich the paper strips. One orifice was designated for the reference electrode, while the other was assigned for the ion recognizing sensor (sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride), as described in the following section.

To prepare the reference electrode, 350 mg of polyvinyl butyral was dissolved in 3 mL of methanol, and the solution was subjected to ultra-sonication for 30 min. Next, 50 mg of sodium chloride and 30 mg of silver nitrate were added to 1.0 mL of the resulting solution, followed by an additional 30 min of ultra-sonication. The cocktail turned white, indicating the formation of silver chloride. Upon exposure to light, the white mixture turned violet, indicating partial reduction to metallic silver. After this color change, 40 μL of the cocktail was carefully applied to the designated orifice on a paper strip and left to dry in the dark for 30 min. Once dried, the polyvinyl butyral film on the paper strip was treated with one drop of 3 M potassium chloride solution and left to stand for 1 h at room temperature. This process was repeated to prepare a total of four sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of solid contact ion sensors
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of solid contact ion sensors, specific materials were utilized for different ions. In a glass petri dish, PVC was combined with the respective recognizing material for each ion: 7 mg of sodium recognizing material (Na-PT) for sodium sensing, 14 mg of potassium recognizing material (K-PT) for potassium sensing, 3.5 mg of calcium recognizing material (Ca-PT) for calcium sensing, and 14 mg of chloride recognizing material (Cl-IP) for chloride sensing. Equal amounts of PVC and plasticizer DOP, 171.5 mg for sodium, 168 mg for potassium, 173.25 mg for calcium, and 168 mg for chloride, were mixed with the recognizing material. The resulting mixture was dissolved in 3 mL of tetrahydrofuran and thoroughly homogenized. A homogeneous cocktail of 30 μL from each mixture was drop-casted onto the designated orifice for each ion sensing application. The drop-casted material was allowed to dry for 30 min leaving behind a dried orifice ready for ion detection.

The effect of different ions, including phosphate, bicarbonate, magnesium, zinc and copper on the response of the sensors described was investigated. To achieve this, the separate solution method was utilized, which involved measuring the potential of a 10−3 M solution of ion of interest and compare that with interfering ions individually where selectivity coefficients were determined by applying the equation [14]:
Log
K =
E 2
− E
1
S
+ log
[
ion
of
interest
]
‐
log
[
J
+ z
]
1 z
Where Log K is the selectivity coefficient, E1 and E2 are the potential of 10−3 M of ion of interest and interfering ion solution
[
J
+ z
]
, respectively, and S is the measured Nernstian slope.

Two calibration curves, matrix-matched and solvent calibration, were prepared for determination of sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride. Matrix-matched calibration standards were prepared by spiking QC samples of every ion with 1 mL of human nasal secretions. The samples were then treated with 3 mL of acetonitrile and centrifuged to remove cellular debris and protein content. The resulting supernatants were dried and reconstituted in 1.5 mL phosphate buffer solution, pH 7.5, and mixed well. The final concentrations of every ion in the matrix-matched calibration standards were in the range of 10−6 to 10−1 M. Potentiometric measurements were conducted using the proposed sensors for the ions under the study as earlier described. The electromotive force values of the samples were recorded and plotted against the logarithm of every ion activity to construct the calibration curves. A solvent calibration curve was prepared and created using the same concentrations of ions in the distilled water.

The proposed method was validated according to the relevant guidelines. The following parameters were evaluated: linearity, accuracy and precision. The accuracy and precision of the described sensors were evaluated by determining the recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD), respectively. The recovery and RSD were determined by analyzing five replicates of the spiked LQC, MQC and HQC samples of every ion in the spiked nasal secretion samples. This was done on the same day for intra-day accuracy and precision, and on three different days for inter-day accuracy and precision. Furthermore, matrix effect was evaluated by examining the interference from endogenous substances of the nasal secretions by applying the following equation:
%
Matrix
effect =
Slope
matrix
calibration
Slope
solvent
calibration
.
100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride in the human nasal secretions of healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory dysfunction

ce:section.ce:para: Subjects were recruited from the ENT Department of Damietta Faculty of Medicine, Al-Azhar University, Egypt, following ethical guidelines. Informed consent was obtained from all participants and the study received approval from the Ethics Committee at Damietta Faculty of Medicine, Al-Azhar University, Egypt. Olfactory function of whole participants was assessed using the universal Sniffin' Sticks test, measuring threshold (T), discrimination (D), identification (I), and TDI scores. A TDI score less than 16.75 indicated anosmia, 16.75 to 30.50 indicated hyposmia, and 30.75 or higher indicated normosmia [15,16]. Then, subjects were divided into two groups. The first group consisted of 30 healthy individuals, aged around 41 years, who were in good general health with normal sense of smell (Sniffin' Sticks TDI score >32). The second group included 30 patients, around 39 years old, who exhibited signs and symptoms of chronic olfactory dysfunction, and their diagnosis was based on clinical examination where the assessed Sniffin' Sticks TDI score was below 16.

Nasal secretion samples were collected by instilling 5 mL of sterile phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.5) into naris of every participant, and the expelled secretions were collected in a plastic petri dish. To denature the proteins in the samples, 3 mL of acetonitrile was added. The samples were then mixed for 1 min and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 35 min. To determine the unknown concentrations of ions in the diluted nasal secretions, the standard addition technique was employed. It was applied by adding known quantities 10−3 M of ions of interest solutions to the samples, which contained unknown concentrations of these elements. Subsequently, the treated samples were analyzed as previously described. The resulting potential change was recorded and used to calculate the ion of interest concentration using the appropriate equation.:
C x
=
C S
(
V s
V x
+
V s
)
[
10
n
(
Δ E
s
)
−
V x
V s
+
V x
]
Where
C x
and
V x
represent the unknown concentration and the volume, respectively,
C S
and
V s
represent the known added ion of interest concentration and its added volume, respectively,
S
is the slope of the respective calibration curve and
Δ E
is the measured change in electromotive force values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the modified paper substrate coated with ink consisted of MWCNT and CQDs
Rational for choosing paper as a platform for the proposed analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Paper is chosen as the platform for detecting sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride ions due to several advantages, including its feasibility as an efficient conductive sensor, easy handling, cost-effectiveness, low sample volume requirement, and compatibility with microfluidic systems [17]. Moreover, it offers a simpler approach for point-of-care clinical outcomes in resource-constrained settings. The sensor design uses two 20 × 5 mm rectangular filter paper strips as the foundation, coated with circular spots of ink containing MWCNT and CQDs. Additionally, two plastic insulator sheets, each with two 4 mm orifices, enclose the paper pieces, with one orifice for the reference electrode and the other for the target ion. Fig. 1 , shows the schematic diagram of the step-by-step sensor design.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transformation of filter paper into conductive platform: enhancing electrical conductivity and performance with nanoparticles inking process
ce:section.ce:para: To transform the insulating filter paper into a conductive platform, it was coated with ink solution containing MWCNT and CQDs. The selection of MWCNTs and CQDs for inclusion in the ink mixture and paper coating process was driven by their synergistically improving for the overall performance of the conductive ink, leading to a more efficient and effective sensor platform compared to other materials. MWCNTs are renowned for their outstanding electrical conductivity, large surface area, and mechanical strength, making them ideal for forming conductive pathways on flexible substrates such as filter paper [18,19]. In addition, CQDs exhibit unique surface chemistry allows for facile functionalization with biomolecules, polymers and other nanomaterials, expanding their versatility in sensing applications [20,21].

In this study, CQDs were prepared using beetroots, a renewable and eco-friendly source. Utilizing beetroot, an abundant natural resource, makes the process sustainable and reduces raw material costs. The method involves straightforward carbonization and simple purification steps, without the need for sophisticated equipment or harsh chemicals. Additionally, beetroot-derived CQDs are biocompatible, making them suitable for sensing applications [22]. During carbonization using a microwave-assisted tool, the high temperature caused sucrose units to dehydrate and break down, leading to the formation of various soluble polymers such as furfural compounds, organic acids, aldehydes, and phenols. Subsequent aldol condensation, cycloaddition, and hydroxyl methyl-mediated furan resin condensation resulted in the formation of CQDs when aromatic clusters appeared and reached a critical super-saturation point, leading to a burst nucleation event [23].

X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy techniques were employed to characterize the synthesized CQDs. The XRD pattern of the synthesized CQDs, Fig. 2 a, demonstrated a sharp diffraction peak (002) at 2θ = 19.25, indicating an interlayer spacing of 0.450 nm, which is attributed to CQDs presence. The narrower and sharper peaks observed in the XRD analysis indicated formation of amorphous nanoparticles with higher degree of crystallinity and improved structural organization in the functionalized CQDs [24].

The morphology and core size of the prepared beetroots-CQDs was examined using TEM. The CQDs exhibited a spherical morphology, as shown in Fig. 2b. The average core sizes of the CQDs were determined using particle size distribution plots (inset, Fig. 2b), which indicated that the formed CQDs had an average core size of 2.0 ± 0.22 nm with a uniform distribution of the spherical shapes and a condensed structure shape. FTIR analysis complemented the XRD and TEM results to identify functional groups on the surface of the beetroots-CQDs, Fig. 2c. The peak at 1630 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the C=O bond, constituting the framework of the carbon nanotubes sidewalls. Bands ranging from 1390 cm−1–1700 cm−1 were attributed to C=C stretching vibrations. A strong C–H stretching was observed at 2930 cm−1, associated with carbon-hydrogen bonds. Additionally, the CQDs displayed a prominent peak at 3440 cm−1, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups on their surfaces, highlighting surface activation [25,26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the coated paper surface characters and the conductivity features
ce:section.ce:para: FESEM was used to evaluate the surface morphology of the paper modified with MWCNT and CQDs, Fig. 3 a, and compared to a conventional paper coated with graphite, Fig. 3b. The FESEM image of the convenient paper exhibited uniform continuous layer of graphite. In contrast, the paper modified with MWCNT and CQDs showed rough heterogeneous surface with widespread network of interconnected MWCNT together with dispersed CQDs that interacted supramolecular with the cellulose fibers of the paper, stabilizing them and forming an effective conductive area, thereby enhancing the paper electrical conductivity.

The effect of MWCNT and CQDs on the paper surface capacity and short-term potential stability was investigated. EIS measurements were done on the paper-based sensor in a 1 × 10−5 M of ion solutions across a frequency range of 1 MHz to 0.1 Hz. The convenient paper substrate exhibited a bulk resistance (Rb) of 315.4 kΩ and a geometrical capacitance (Cg) of 3.7 nF in the high-frequency region. Conversely, the paper modified with MWCNT and CQDs showed decreased values of Rb (218.4 kΩ) and Cg (2.9 nF), indicating enhanced conductivity and improved charge storage capacity. At the low-frequency range, the double-layer capacitance of the sensor increased from 10.8 to 25.8 μF following the coating with MWCNT and CQDs. This substantial increase in the double-layer capacitance demonstrates the improved transduction properties and indicates potential stability. The EIS-generated Nyquist plot, Fig. 3c, presents an evidence of the improved performance of the developed coated paper substrate, clearly illustrating a significant impedance reduction compared to the convenient substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the components of the proposed sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity and selectivity of sensors are known to be dependent not only on the nature of ion recognizing materials, but also significantly on the composition of the sensing ingredients [27]. Therefore, it was of interest to study the effects of the sensing ingredients including ion exchanger and type of plasticizer on the potential response of the proposed sensor.

Sodium, potassium and calcium are cations that interact with an anionic ion exchanger, phosphotungstic acid, to prepare water insoluble association complex of these ions (Na-PT, K-PT and Ca-PT). The resulting precipitates have low solubility product and physically compatible with the matrix. The ratio of sodium and potassium to the ion exchanger in the formed complex was found to be 1:1 as proven by the obtained Nernstian slope (59.21, 59.51 mV/decade respectively). While the ratio of calcium to the ion exchanger in the formed complex was found to be 2:1 as proven by the obtained Nernstian slope (29.44 mV/decade). The prepared ion exchangers, Na-PT, K-PT and Ca-PT were studied by varying the percentages of the ion exchangers, while keeping the percentages of the PVC and the plasticizer equal 1:1 as shown in Table 1 . The sensors made of 2 % (w/w) Na-PT, 4 % (w/w) K-PT and 1 % (w/w) Ca-PT exhibited the best performance.

On the other hand, chloride is as an anion that reacts with cationic exchanger, iron(II)– phenanthroline, in a basic medium and forms water insoluble ion association complex (Cl-IP). The ratio of chloride to the ion exchanger in the formed complex was found to be 1:1 as proven by the obtained Nernstian slope (59.18 mV/decade). The prepared ion exchanger, Cl-IP was studied by varying the percentages of the ion exchangers, while keeping the percentages of the PVC and the plasticizer equal 1:1 as shown in Table 1. The sensors made of 4 % (w/w) Cl-IP exhibited the best performance.

Plasticizers are essential components in the design of sensors, facilitating enhanced sensitivity and selectivity to specific analytes. They improve interaction with the sensing material and allow for higher sensitivity to low concentrations of the target ion. Plasticizer should be compatible with PVC and the ion exchanger with low solubility in aqueous solution [28]. In exploration for a suitable plasticizer, we used two plasticizers, DOP and TBP as shown in Table 1. The sensors plasticized with DOP produced the best response. It is likely due to relatively high molecular weight, low dielectric constant and high lipophilic characters that make it more suitable for the investigated sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation of the described sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The sensors ability to determine sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride, in spiked nasal secretions was evaluated based on Nernstian slope, dynamic range, detection limit, and response time. The sensor showed a linear correlation between the measured potential and the ions activity over a concentration range of 10−6 to 10−1 M, exhibiting Nernstian behavior with slopes of 59.21, 59.51, 29.44, 59.18 for sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride respectively. The potential remained stable at pH values ranging from 2 to 9 with a dynamic response time of 30 s for the whole proposed sensors. The Nernstian behaviors were stable over 180 days, with no more than a 1 mV variation on any given day or day-to-day. The performance and results achieved by the proposed sensors are summarized in Table 2 .

Selectivity in an analytical method refers to the capability to accurately identify and quantify specific ions within complex mixtures or matrices, while minimizing interference from other components with similar behavior. The selectivity coefficient is a crucial metric that quantifies the extent to which interfering ions affect the sensor's response to the primary ion. Lower selectivity coefficients demonstrate higher selectivity, signifying that the sensor is less influenced by interferences. In the current study, the separate solution method was employed to determine the selectivity coefficients for ions expected to influence the proposed sensors. This method involves measuring the sensor's potential in separate solutions of the primary ion and each interfering ion. The selectivity coefficients were calculated using the Nicolsky-Eisenman equation, which relates the potential differences to the activities of the ions [14]. The results, presented in Table 2, show very small selectivity coefficients, indicating minimal interference from other ions. This high selectivity underscores the effectiveness of the sensors in accurately determining sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride ions. The data also demonstrated a Nernstian dependence on the primary ion over a wide concentration range, further validating the sensors' performance. The Nernstian response indicates that the sensor's potential changes predictably with the logarithm of the ion concentration, ensuring reliable and reproducible measurements.

Potentiometric measurements were conducted using the proposed sensors. Two calibration curves were generated for every sensor: solvent calibration and matrix-matched calibration. This approach is generally utilized for sensors in the biological matrix, as it minimizes the matrix effects and background signals of the sample. For the solvent calibration curves, the sensors were immersed in the corresponding ion solutions ranging from 10−6 to 10−1 M. After 30 s of equilibration with stirring, the electromotive force values were recorded and plotted against the logarithm of ions activity. In addition, matrix-matched calibration standards were prepared by spiking QC samples of sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride stock solutions into 1 mL of human nasal secretions. The samples were treated with acetonitrile, centrifuged to remove cellular debris and mucus protein content, and the resulting supernatants were dried and dissolved in phosphate buffer solution at pH 7.5. The electromotive force values of these samples were recorded and plotted against the logarithm of ions activity to construct the matrix calibration curve. The slopes of the matrix-matched calibration curve and the solvent calibration curve were compared and found to be in the range of 100.5–102 %, which indicated that the method was unaffected by endogenous components of nasal matrix. The sensors showed a linear correlation between the measured potential and the ions activity over a concentration range of 10−6 to 10−1 M for the investigated ions.

Accuracy and precision were evaluated by analyzing low, medium, and high quality control (LQC, MQC, HQC) samples in five replicates. These analyses were carried out on the same day and were repeated on three different days. The results, as presented in Table 2, met the accepted criteria for accuracy and precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the proposed method for determination of sodium, potassium, calcium and chloride in the nasal secretions of healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory dysfunction
ce:section.ce:para: The paper-based sensors were utilized to determine sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride in the human nasal secretions from healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory dysfunction. To achieve this, known volumes of a 10−3 M standard solution of the target ion were added to aliquots of diluted nasal secretions containing unknown concentration of the ions under the study. The resulting potential change was recorded and used to calculate the ions concentration in the sample solution using the equation mentioned in the experimental section. Table 3 presents a comparative analysis of sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride between the patients with chronic olfactory dysfunction and the healthy control group. The results indicated the presence of investigated ions in the nasal mucus of both groups. Furthermore, there was significant higher levels of calcium observed in patients with olfactory dysfunction compared to the healthy group. No significant differences in the measured sodium, potassium, and chloride levels between the healthy individuals and patients with the olfactory dysfunction.

In this study, cost-effective paper-based sensors were developed and modified with multi-walled carbon nanotubes and carbon quantum dots derived from beetroots, for the selective detection of sodium, potassium, calcium, and chloride ions in human nasal secretions. These sensors exhibited Nernstian behavior with high sensitivity and selectivity, offering promising potential for the diagnosis of olfactory dysfunction. Application of the developed sensors in clinical samples from healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory dysfunction revealed a significant elevation in calcium levels in the nasal secretions of individuals with olfactory dysfunction. This suggests a potential diagnostic relevance of elevated calcium levels as a marker for olfactory dysfunction.
This research was funded by Taif University, Saudi Arabia, Project No. (TU-DSPP-2024-154).

Ahmed H. Abdelazim: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Mohamed H. Abdelazim: Validation, Investigation. Majed A. Algarni: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Fahad T. Alsulami: Writing – review & editing. Reem M. Alnemari: Visualization, Formal analysis. Adnan Alharbi: Investigation, Conceptualization. Maram H. Abduljabbar: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Atiah H. Almalki: Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relations that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to Taif University, Saudi Arabia, for supporting this work through Project No. (TU-DSPP-2024-154).
